title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 2: The Yoga of Knowledge
description: Chapter 2 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna teaches Arjuna the immortality of the soul and the discipline of selfless action.
introduction:
Chapter 2 of the Bhagavad Gita — the Yoga of Knowledge (Sankhya Yoga) — is the philosophical foundation of the entire work. After Arjuna collapses in despair on the battlefield, refusing to fight his own kinsmen, Krishna delivers the core teaching: the Self (atman) is eternal and indestructible; the body dies but the soul does not; therefore there is no cause for grief. From this metaphysical foundation, Krishna introduces the discipline of selfless action (karma yoga): act according to duty, without attachment to results.

Verse 2.47 — karmaṇy evādhikāras te ('you have the right to action alone') — is the most quoted verse in the Gita and one of the most influential ethical statements in world literature.

translation.original_text: śrī-bhagavān uvāca: aśocyān anvaśocas tvaṃ prajñā-vādāṃś ca bhāṣase | gatāsūn agatāsūṃś ca nānuśocanti paṇḍitāḥ || na tv evāhaṃ jātu nāsaṃ na tvaṃ neme janādhipāḥ | na caiva na bhaviṣyāmaḥ sarve vayam ataḥ param || dehino 'smin yathā dehe kaumāraṃ yauvanaṃ jarā | tathā dehāntara-prāptir dhīras tatra na muhyati ||
translation.text: The Blessed Lord said: You grieve for those who should not be grieved for, yet you speak words of wisdom. The wise grieve neither for the living nor for the dead. Never was there a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor these kings of men. Nor will there be a time hereafter when any of us shall cease to be. As the embodied soul passes through childhood, youth, and old age in this body, so too it passes to another body. The wise person is not bewildered by this.

translation.original_text: avināśi tu tad viddhi yena sarvam idaṃ tatam | vināśam avyayasyāsya na kaścit kartum arhati || antavanta ime dehā nityasyoktāḥ śarīriṇaḥ | anāśino 'prameyasya tasmād yudhyasva bhārata || ya enaṃ vetti hantāraṃ yaś cainaṃ manyate hatam | ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṃ hanti na hanyate || na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin nāyaṃ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ | ajo nityaḥ śāśvato 'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre ||
translation.text: Know that to be indestructible by which all this is pervaded. No one can bring about the destruction of the imperishable. These bodies of the embodied one are said to have an end, but the embodied one itself is eternal, indestructible, and immeasurable — therefore fight, O Bharata. One who thinks this one is the slayer, and one who thinks this one is slain — both of them do not understand. This one does not slay, nor is it slain. It is never born and never dies. Having once existed, it does not cease to be. Unborn, eternal, everlasting, primeval — it is not slain when the body is slain.

translation.original_text: karmaṇy evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana | mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi || yoga-sthaḥ kuru karmāṇi saṅgaṃ tyaktvā dhanaṃjaya | siddhy-asiddhyoḥ samo bhūtvā samatvaṃ yoga ucyate ||
translation.text: You have the right to action alone, never to its fruits. Let not the fruit of action be your motive, nor let your attachment be to inaction. Established in yoga, perform actions, O Dhananjaya, having abandoned attachment, remaining equal in success and failure. This equanimity is called yoga.

translation.original_text: arjuna uvāca: sthita-prajñasya kā bhāṣā samādhi-sthasya keśava | sthita-dhīḥ kiṃ prabhāṣeta kim āsīta vrajeta kim ||
translation.text: Arjuna said: What is the description of one who is steady in wisdom, who is established in deep meditation, O Keshava? How does the one of steady mind speak? How does he sit? How does he walk? The Blessed Lord said: When a person abandons all desires of the mind, O Partha, and is satisfied in the Self by the Self alone, then he is said to be of steady wisdom. He whose mind is untroubled by sorrow, who does not crave pleasure, who is free from attachment, fear, and anger — he is called a sage of steady mind. He who is without attachment on every side, who does not rejoice or recoil on obtaining good or evil — his wisdom is firmly established.

translation.original_text: dhyāyato viṣayān puṃsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate | saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho 'bhijāyate || krodhād bhavati saṃmohaḥ saṃmohāt smṛti-vibhramaḥ | smṛti-bhraṃśād buddhi-nāśo buddhi-nāśāt praṇaśyati ||
translation.text: When a person dwells on the objects of the senses, attachment to them arises. From attachment is born desire. From desire arises anger. From anger comes delusion. From delusion comes the loss of memory. From the loss of memory comes the destruction of intelligence. And from the destruction of intelligence one perishes. But one who moves among sense objects with the senses under control, free from attraction and aversion, attains serenity. In serenity, all sorrows are destroyed, for the intelligence of the serene-minded quickly becomes steady.
